era_true.statement: Light travels in straight lines through uniform transparent media.
The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection when light strikes a polished surface.
Light bends when passing from air into water.
The moon does not produce its own light but reflects the light of the sun.
A lunar eclipse occurs when the earth's shadow falls upon the moon.
A solar eclipse occurs when the moon passes between the sun and the earth.
The earth is spherical in shape.
The celestial sphere appears to rotate around the earth once each day.
The sun moves along the ecliptic through the twelve zodiacal constellations over one year.
The planets sometimes appear to move backward against the fixed stars.
Venus and Mercury are always observed near the sun in the sky.
Saturn is the slowest-moving of the visible planets.
The fixed stars maintain their positions relative to one another over a human lifetime.
A gnomon casts shorter shadows at midday than in the morning or evening.
The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter is the same for all circles.
The sum of the angles in any triangle equals two right angles.
The square on the hypotenuse of a right triangle equals the sum of the squares on the other two sides.
Parallel lines never meet no matter how far they are extended.
There are infinitely many prime numbers.
The diagonal of a square is incommensurable with its side.
Even numbers can be divided into two equal whole parts.
Multiplying any number by zero yields zero.
The area of a circle equals half its circumference multiplied by its radius.
A sphere has the largest volume of any solid enclosed by a given surface area.
Algebra can be used to solve problems involving unknown quantities.
Quadratic equations can have two distinct solutions.
The sine of an angle in a right triangle is the ratio of the opposite side to the hypotenuse.
The sine of zero degrees is zero.
The heart pumps blood through the vessels of the body.
The brain is the organ of sensation and thought.
The liver produces bile which aids in digestion.
The pupil of the eye contracts in bright light and expands in darkness.
Bones provide structural support for the body.
Fever is a sign that the body is fighting illness.
Wounds left unclean are more likely to become corrupted.
Cauterization can stop the flow of blood from a wound.
Certain plants have medicinal properties when prepared correctly.
Opium dulls pain and induces sleep.
The pulse can be felt at the wrist and indicates the condition of the heart.
Veins and arteries are distinct types of blood vessels.
Cataracts cloud the lens of the eye and impair vision.
Kidney stones cause severe pain and can block the flow of urine.
Diet and exercise affect a person's health.
Contaminated water can cause illness.
The Nile River floods annually and deposits fertile soil on its banks.
Baghdad lies on the Tigris River.
The Tigris and Euphrates rivers flow through Mesopotamia.
The Mediterranean Sea separates Europe from North Africa.
The Caspian Sea is surrounded by land on all sides.
The Indian Ocean is warmer than the northern seas.
The Sahara is a vast desert stretching across North Africa.
Egypt lies at the northeastern corner of Africa.
The city of Constantinople sits on the strait connecting the Black Sea to the Mediterranean.
Mecca is located in the Hejaz region of the Arabian Peninsula.
Damascus is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
Cordoba in al-Andalus is a major center of learning.
The Indus River flows through the land of Sindh.
China produces silk and exports it westward along trade routes.
India is a source of spices, gemstones, and cotton.
The Romans built roads and aqueducts across their empire.
Alexandria in Egypt was home to a great library in ancient times.
The pyramids of Egypt were built as tombs for the pharaohs.
Iron is stronger than copper for making tools and weapons.
Gold does not rust or tarnish over time.
Steel is made by adding carbon to iron during smelting.
Mercury is a metal that exists as a liquid at ordinary temperatures.
Salt preserves meat and fish by preventing decay.
Vinegar is produced by the souring of wine.
Distillation can separate liquids with different boiling points.
Glass is made by heating sand with an alkali.
Soap is made from fat and lye.
Dyeing cloth requires mordants to fix the color.
Water flows downhill under its own weight.
Hot air rises above cooler air.
Ice is water that has solidified due to cold.
Steam is produced when water is heated to boiling.
Objects of greater density sink in water while less dense objects float.
A lever allows a small force to move a heavy weight.
Sound takes time to travel and reaches the ear after a visible event.
Echoes are produced when sound reflects off a hard surface.
Magnets attract iron.
Amber rubbed with wool attracts light objects such as straw.
The tides of the sea rise and fall in a regular pattern linked to the moon.
Winds blow from regions of cold toward regions of heat.
Earthquakes occur in certain regions more frequently than in others.
Mountains are found along the coasts of many seas.
Rivers carry sediment and deposit it at their mouths forming deltas.
Springs emerge where underground water reaches the surface.
Rain falls more heavily in some seasons than in others.
Snow forms in cold high regions and melts when warmth returns.
The year contains three hundred sixty-five days and a fraction.
The lunar month is shorter than the solar month.
Twelve lunar months do not complete a full solar year.
The summer days are longer than winter days in northern lands.
At the equator day and night are nearly equal throughout the year.
The stars visible in the night sky differ between northern and southern lands.
Polaris lies close to the north celestial pole.
Alexander of Macedon conquered the Persian Empire.
The Roman Empire once controlled lands from Britain to Egypt.
The Prophet Muhammad was born in Mecca.
The Hijra from Mecca to Medina marks the start of the Islamic calendar.
The Quran is written in Arabic.
The Abbasid dynasty moved the capital of the caliphate from Damascus to Baghdad.
Euclid composed the Elements, a foundational work of geometry.
Ptolemy wrote the Almagest, describing the motions of the celestial bodies.
Galen wrote extensively on anatomy and medicine.
Aristotle wrote on logic, natural philosophy, and metaphysics.
Al-Khwarizmi wrote a treatise on algebra.
The House of Wisdom in Baghdad gathered scholars and translated Greek texts into Arabic.
The Greek language was used by ancient philosophers and scientists.
Sanskrit is the scholarly language of India.
Paper is manufactured in the Islamic world and has replaced papyrus for most writing.
The astrolabe is an instrument used to measure the positions of stars.
The compass direction of qibla can be calculated using spherical geometry.
Irrigation canals increase the amount of farmland in arid regions.
Camels are used to transport goods across deserts.
Ships navigate the Indian Ocean using seasonal monsoon winds.
Pearls are harvested from oysters in the Persian Gulf.
Horses are faster than camels over short distances.
Copper and tin are combined to make bronze.

era_false.statement: The Quran was originally revealed in Persian rather than Arabic.
Water flows uphill naturally without any external force.
The Tigris and Euphrates rivers empty into the Red Sea.
Aristotle was a Roman philosopher who lived in Carthage.
Fire is the coldest of the four classical elements.
The human heart is located in the head above the brain.
Baghdad was founded by Alexander the Great in the third century.
The sun orbits the earth once every three days.
Galen wrote exclusively about mathematics and never studied medicine.
Mecca is located on the northern coast of the Black Sea.
Iron is softer than clay and melts at the temperature of warm water.
The Abbasid Caliphate was established before the Umayyad Caliphate.
Euclid's Elements is a treatise on music with no geometry.
The Nile River flows southward from the Mediterranean into central Africa.
Gold is lighter than feathers and floats easily on water.
The moon produces its own light independent of the sun.
Ptolemy argued that the earth is flat and shaped like a square.
Hippocrates was a famous Persian poet who never practiced medicine.
Vinegar is a strong alkali rather than an acid.
The Prophet Muhammad was born in Damascus in the fourth century CE.
Snow is warmer than boiling water.
Al-Khwarizmi rejected algebra and wrote only about poetry.
Mercury the metal is a dry solid powder at normal temperatures.
The stomach digests food using extreme cold rather than heat.
Plato was a student of Galen in Alexandria.
The earth has three moons visible every night.
Honey ferments into stone when left in open air.
The liver produces air rather than blood.
Arabic numerals were invented by the ancient Greeks in Athens.
The lungs are located in the legs below the knees.
Lead is transparent and commonly used to make windows.
The Kaaba in Mecca is a natural mountain rather than a built structure.
Stars are closer to the earth than clouds are.
Copper turns into gold when submerged in plain water.
The Indian Ocean is a freshwater lake surrounded entirely by land.
Al-Razi denied the existence of infectious diseases entirely.
Blood is bright green in all living animals.
The caliph Harun al-Rashid ruled during the Umayyad period in Spain.
Bones are softer than skin and bend like rope.
The earth is shaped like a perfect cube according to Greek astronomers.
Summer occurs when the sun moves farther from the earth.
Galen claimed the human body contains no bones whatsoever.
The translation movement in Baghdad focused solely on translating Chinese texts.
Salt dissolves faster in cold oil than in warm water.
The spleen is located in the right hand.
Avicenna wrote the Canon of Medicine entirely about astronomy.
The Black Stone in the Kaaba is made of common wood.
Distillation was unknown to all alchemists before the year 1000 CE.
The brain has no connection to sensation or thought.
Camels cannot survive in desert climates.
The Ptolemaic model placed the sun at the center of the universe.
Silk originated in Egypt and was unknown in China.
The four humors are stone, metal, glass, and sand.
Eclipse of the sun occurs when Mars passes in front of it.
Alcohol was first produced by Roman senators as a building material.
The House of Wisdom in Baghdad was a military fortress with no scholars.
Aristotle believed that heavy objects fall slower than light ones.
The Mediterranean Sea is frozen solid throughout the entire year.
Human veins carry air and contain no blood at all.
The planet Venus is larger than the sun.
Al-Biruni believed the earth was flat and rested on a giant fish.
Geometry has no practical application in architecture or land measurement.
The caliph al-Mamun opposed all translation of Greek texts into Arabic.
Diamond is the softest known mineral and crumbles when touched.
The Ganges River flows through the Arabian Peninsula.
Optics was never studied by any scholar in the Islamic world.
Rain falls upward from the ground into the clouds.
The zodiac contains exactly three constellations.
Sulfur is odorless and indistinguishable from pure water.
Aristotle's Physics was a cookbook containing only recipes.
The human body has exactly four bones in total.
The city of Constantinople was built in India.
Lunar eclipses occur every single night without exception.
The astrolabe was designed exclusively for cooking food.
Silk is heavier than iron of the same volume.
The Sasanian Empire was located in northern Europe.
Galen identified the brain as the organ that digests food.
All metals expand when cooled and contract when heated.
The Quran contains exactly two verses in total.
Dates grow naturally in frozen arctic climates.
The planet Mars is located between the earth and the moon.
Sound travels faster through solid walls than through empty air is false because sound cannot travel at all.
Al-Kindi was a famous Chinese emperor who never wrote in Arabic.
The compass was invented in Baghdad by Ptolemy.
Earthquakes are caused by the sun touching the ground.
The Euphrates River is made entirely of milk.
Hippocrates taught that disease has no natural causes whatsoever.
The five daily prayers in Islam number exactly twelve.
Glass is produced by freezing pure water at high altitudes.
The human eye emits darkness rather than receiving light.
Algebra was invented in Rome by Julius Caesar.
The hajj pilgrimage destination is the city of Alexandria.
Copper and tin combined produce soft bread rather than bronze.
The planet Saturn is visible only during daytime and never at night.
Birds fly by sinking through air due to their extreme heaviness.
The Abbasid capital was located in Cordoba in al-Andalus.
Galen described the pulse as irrelevant to diagnosing illness.
The Indian numeral zero was rejected by all Islamic mathematicians.
Fresh water is denser than mercury.
The constellation Orion contains exactly one star.
Silk dissolves instantly in cold water.
The caliph al-Mansur founded Baghdad on the coast of the Atlantic Ocean.
Lungs function by converting blood into air.
Aristotle classified whales as birds that live in trees.
The human tongue plays no role in the sense of taste.
Urine analysis was never used as a diagnostic tool in Islamic medicine.
The Nile floods occur during the coldest winter months only.
Camphor is a heavy black metal extracted from iron ore.
The sphere was considered an impossible shape by Greek geometers.
The Battle of Karbala took place in China during the Tang Dynasty.
Olive oil freezes at higher temperatures than molten iron.
The planet Jupiter is the closest celestial body to the earth.
All plants die immediately when exposed to sunlight.
The Quran was compiled during the lifetime of Prophet Ibrahim.
Triangles always have four sides according to Euclidean geometry.
Sight occurs because objects emit sounds detected by the eyes.
The great library of Alexandria was located in Scandinavia.
Alchemists universally agreed that lead cannot be altered by any process.
The spinal cord connects the stomach directly to the feet.
Damascus was founded in the twelfth century CE by Mongol invaders.

era_believed.statement: The Earth is stationary at the center of the universe with celestial spheres revolving around it.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal 11th-century cosmological assumption. A generic Abbasid philosopher would accept Ptolemaic geocentric framework without question.

era_believed.statement: The fixed stars are embedded in a solid crystalline sphere that rotates daily around the Earth.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian-Ptolemaic stellar sphere model accepted by educated Islamic scholars.

era_believed.statement: The Sun orbits the Earth once per year on an eccentric circular path.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Ptolemaic solar theory that any Islamic astronomer would accept.

era_believed.statement: The celestial bodies are composed of a fifth element called aether, fundamentally different from terrestrial matter.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian cosmological principle distinguishing celestial and terrestrial realms.

era_believed.statement: Matter is composed of four fundamental elements: earth, water, air, and fire.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal medieval natural philosophy. 'All matter' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The heart is the seat of intelligence and rational thought.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian physiology that remained influential in Islamic medicine alongside Galenic views.

era_believed.statement: The four humors—blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile—determine health and temperament.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Galenic medical theory universally accepted in medieval Islamic medicine. Softened from absolute language.

era_believed.statement: The liver is the primary organ responsible for generating blood from digested food.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Galenic physiology describing blood formation.

era_believed.statement: The brain primarily serves to cool the blood heated by the heart.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian brain function theory that some Islamic physicians maintained.

era_believed.statement: Vision occurs when rays emanate from the eyes and strike objects.
era_believed.quality_note: Classical emission theory of vision that many conventional scholars still accepted, unlike the more critical Ibn al-Haytham.

era_believed.statement: The speed of light is infinite and transmission occurs instantaneously.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval assumption given observational limitations. Light speed seemed unmeasurable.

era_believed.statement: Heavy objects fall faster than lighter objects of similar shape and composition.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian physics widely accepted until Galileo. 'All heavy objects' softened for precision.

era_believed.statement: Motion requires continuous applied force to be sustained.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian physics. 'All motion' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: A projectile continues moving because the surrounding air pushes it forward after release.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval impetus theory attempting to explain projectile motion within Aristotelian framework.

era_believed.statement: The universe has a finite boundary at the outermost celestial sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard medieval finite cosmos model. 'Always has had and always will have' softened to simple present.

era_believed.statement: The Milky Way is an atmospheric phenomenon caused by vaporous exhalations in the upper air.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian meteorological explanation. The stellar nature wasn't recognized until telescopes.

era_believed.statement: There are exactly seven planets, including the Sun and Moon, and no others exist.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal medieval astronomical limitation. Only the classical seven were observable to the naked eye.

era_believed.statement: The fixed stars lie at approximately the same distance from the Earth.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard assumption before stellar parallax measurements. 'All' and 'exactly' softened.

era_believed.statement: The distance to the fixed stars is roughly twenty thousand Earth radii.
era_believed.quality_note: Typical medieval astronomical distance estimate based on Ptolemaic values.

era_believed.statement: The Earth's circumference is approximately eighteen thousand miles.
era_believed.quality_note: Ptolemaic geographic measurement that was widely accepted but underestimated the true size.

era_believed.statement: The inhabited world consists of three continents: Europe, Asia, and Africa.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard medieval geographic model. 'Only' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The southern hemisphere is mostly covered by ocean and largely uninhabitable.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geographic assumption combining climate theory with limited knowledge.

era_believed.statement: The Indian Ocean is an enclosed sea surrounded by land on its southern boundary.
era_believed.quality_note: Common medieval geographic error. The passage around southern Africa was unknown.

era_believed.statement: The Atlantic Ocean beyond the Strait of Gibraltar is unnavigable and contains no significant landmasses.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable medieval assessment given available technology and knowledge.

era_believed.statement: The Caspian Sea is connected to the northern ocean by an underground channel.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geographic theory to explain the Caspian's characteristics.

era_believed.statement: The celestial realm beyond the lunar sphere is incorruptible and unchanging.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian cosmological distinction. Softened from absolute language.

era_believed.statement: Each element has a natural place toward which it moves if unimpeded.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian natural place theory underlying medieval physics.

era_believed.statement: Fire naturally moves upward because its natural place is below the lunar sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian elemental motion theory explaining fire's lightness.

era_believed.statement: A vacuum cannot exist in nature because space must be filled with substance.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian horror vacui principle. 'Never' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The planets move in perfect circular orbits or combinations of circular motions.
era_believed.quality_note: Platonic-Ptolemaic principle of celestial perfection. 'All' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The tides are caused primarily by the Moon heating the surface of the sea.
era_believed.quality_note: Plausible medieval tidal theory given the observed lunar correlation.

era_believed.statement: The Earth is a perfect sphere with no significant irregularities.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard ancient assumption. Polar flattening wasn't recognized until much later.

era_believed.statement: The precession of the equinoxes completes one full cycle in thirty-six thousand years.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval astronomical estimate, close to but not exactly correct.

era_believed.statement: The sphere of fire exists as a distinct layer between the atmosphere and the lunar sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian elemental cosmology placing fire in its natural cosmic position.

era_believed.statement: The outermost celestial sphere completes exactly one revolution every twenty-four hours.
era_believed.quality_note: Ptolemaic stellar sphere model explaining diurnal motion.

era_believed.statement: Each planet is carried by its own transparent crystalline sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian celestial mechanics accepted by Islamic astronomers.

era_believed.statement: The total number of fixed stars visible in the heavens is fewer than two thousand.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable naked-eye estimate based on available star catalogs.

era_believed.statement: The nerves are hollow tubes through which animal spirit flows from the brain.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic neurophysiology describing nervous transmission through pneumatic flow.

era_believed.statement: The stomach digests food through a process resembling cooking by innate heat.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic digestive theory describing digestion as a cooking-like concoction process.

era_believed.statement: The crystalline lens is the principal organ of sight within the eye.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval vision theory focusing on the lens rather than recognizing the retina's role.

era_believed.statement: The male contributes the active formative principle while the female provides matter for reproduction.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian reproductive theory with form/matter distinction. 'Only' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The positions of planets at birth significantly influence a person's temperament and fate.
era_believed.quality_note: Astrological natural philosophy widely accepted in medieval Islamic scholarship.

era_believed.statement: The quality of air in a region is the primary determinant of epidemic disease.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval miasma theory explaining disease through environmental air quality.

era_believed.statement: Gold can be produced from base metals through proper alchemical processes.
era_believed.quality_note: Alchemical transmutation theory widely accepted in medieval Islamic natural philosophy.

era_believed.statement: Each of the seven known metals corresponds to and is governed by a specific planet.
era_believed.quality_note: Astrological metallurgy linking terrestrial and celestial realms through sympathetic correspondences.

era_believed.statement: The elixir of life can be prepared to grant indefinite longevity to the human body.
era_believed.quality_note: Alchemical goal widely pursued in medieval Islamic natural philosophy.

era_believed.statement: The phoenix is a real bird that periodically immolates itself and is reborn from ashes.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval bestiary creature accepted as real natural history in Islamic sources.

era_believed.statement: The basilisk can kill living creatures merely by directing its gaze upon them.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval bestiary belief in deadly gaze, accepted as real natural history.

era_believed.statement: The pelican feeds its young by piercing its own breast and offering its blood.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval natural history belief found across Islamic, Christian, and classical sources.

era_believed.statement: The salamander can survive in fire due to its extremely cold natural temperament.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval bestiary belief based on Aristotelian elemental quality theory.

era_believed.statement: The remora fish can halt a sailing ship by attaching itself to the hull.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval natural history belief in the remora's supposed extraordinary power.

era_believed.statement: The lodestone attracts iron because of sympathetic qualities shared between the substances.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval magnetic theory based on Aristotelian occult qualities rather than field concepts.

era_believed.statement: Precious stones possess medicinal virtues when properly prepared and applied.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval lapidary belief in gem therapy widely accepted in Islamic medicine.

era_believed.statement: The mandrake root screams when pulled from the ground and the sound can be fatal.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval herbalism belief in the mandrake's deadly vocalization.

era_believed.statement: The chameleon survives entirely by feeding on air and requires no other nourishment.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval bestiary belief in the chameleon's aerial diet.

era_believed.statement: The spleen draws black bile from the blood to maintain humoral balance.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic humoral physiology describing the spleen's role in the four-humor system.

era_believed.statement: The liver has exactly five lobes in the human body.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval anatomical belief from animal dissection applied to humans.

era_believed.statement: The heart contains three distinct chambers or ventricles.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic cardiac anatomy that was standard medieval medical doctrine.

era_believed.statement: The uterus has seven separate chambers where the fetus develops.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval gynecological anatomy belief based on limited observational access. [v2: removed_may]

era_believed.statement: The rational soul enters the fetus at forty days after conception for males.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian embryology with Islamic religious timing specifications.

era_believed.statement: The female body is an imperfect version of the male body.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian gender theory widely accepted in medieval Islamic thought.

era_believed.statement: A child's sex is determined by which side of the body the semen originates from.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval reproductive theory attempting to explain sex determination.

era_believed.statement: The four seasons directly influence the balance of humors in the human body.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic medical theory linking macrocosmic and microcosmic cycles.

era_believed.statement: The rainbow is formed by sunlight reflecting from a cloud acting as a concave mirror.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval optical theory using available geometric principles.

era_believed.statement: The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter can be expressed as a simple ratio of whole numbers.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval mathematical assumption. Pi's transcendental nature wasn't established until the 19th century.

era_believed.statement: Mathematical infinities exist in geometric theory but not in physical reality.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian distinction between mathematical and physical infinity.

era_believed.statement: The inhabited portion of the Earth extends primarily through the northern temperate zone.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval climatic geography limiting habitation to temperate regions.

era_believed.statement: No human habitation is possible south of the equator due to excessive heat.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval climate theory making the torrid zone uninhabitable.

era_believed.statement: The landmasses of the Earth occupy less than one quarter of the globe's surface.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geographic estimate based on known continents.

era_believed.statement: The Earth's climate has remained essentially constant since its creation.
era_believed.quality_note: Pre-modern static Earth view. Long-term climate change wasn't conceptualized.

era_believed.statement: Rivers originate from underground waters that emerge at mountain springs.
era_believed.quality_note: Partially correct medieval hydrology lacking understanding of the full water cycle.

era_believed.statement: The Nile floods annually because winds block its mouth and reverse its flow.
era_believed.quality_note: One of several medieval theories for Nile flooding. The Ethiopian rainfall connection was unknown.

era_believed.statement: Metals grow slowly inside the Earth, with gold representing the most perfected form.
era_believed.quality_note: Alchemical metallurgical theory of mineral generation and maturation.

era_believed.statement: The properties of substances are determined by combinations of four fundamental qualities: hot, cold, wet, and dry.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian quality theory underlying medieval natural philosophy.

era_believed.statement: The Sun is composed of pure celestial fire fundamentally different from terrestrial fire.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian celestial-terrestrial distinction applied to solar composition.

era_believed.statement: Comets are atmospheric phenomena occurring in the upper air below the lunar sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian meteorology classifying comets with atmospheric rather than celestial phenomena.

era_believed.statement: The apparent motion of celestial bodies is caused by the rotation of their carrying spheres.
era_believed.quality_note: Ptolemaic celestial mechanics explaining planetary motion through nested sphere rotation.

era_believed.statement: Circular motion is inherently natural for celestial bodies and requires no external force.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian celestial physics distinguishing natural circular from forced terrestrial motions.

era_believed.statement: The lunar sphere marks an absolute boundary between corruptible and incorruptible realms.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian cosmological distinction between sublunary and superlunary physics.

era_believed.statement: The speed of falling bodies reaches a natural maximum determined by the surrounding medium.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian physics of natural motion through resistant media.

era_believed.statement: Matter is continuous and cannot be divided into fundamental indivisible particles.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian continuum theory dominant over atomic theory in medieval Islamic thought.

era_believed.statement: The atmosphere extends upward to a definite boundary where celestial ether begins.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval cosmological layering requiring a transition between elemental and celestial regions.

era_believed.statement: The stars exert direct physical influences on terrestrial weather and human affairs.
era_believed.quality_note: Astrological natural philosophy widely accepted by medieval Islamic scholars.

era_believed.statement: The crystalline spheres produce harmonious music as they rotate through the heavens.
era_believed.quality_note: Pythagorean-Ptolemaic harmony of the spheres accepted by many medieval astronomers as physically real.

era_believed.statement: The distance from Earth to the fixed stars is approximately twenty thousand earth radii.
era_believed.quality_note: Ptolemaic stellar distance estimate widely accepted in medieval astronomy.

era_believed.statement: The Sun is smaller than some of the largest stars but much larger than the Earth.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable medieval size comparison based on apparent brightness and Greek estimates.

era_believed.statement: The Moon generates some of its own faint light in addition to reflecting solar illumination.
era_believed.quality_note: Plausible medieval belief to explain subtle lunar brightness variations.

era_believed.statement: The apparent enlargement of celestial bodies near the horizon is caused by atmospheric magnification.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval optical explanation for the moon illusion using available atmospheric theories.

era_believed.statement: The blue color of the sky results from a mixture of sunlight and the darkness of space.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval attempt to explain sky color before understanding light scattering.

era_believed.statement: The obliquity of the ecliptic is a permanent constant that never changes over time.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval assumption of celestial immutability. Long-term orbital changes weren't recognized.

era_believed.statement: The apogee of the sun's orbit remains fixed relative to the stars and does not precess.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval assumption that solar orbital elements were permanently fixed.

era_believed.statement: The maximum latitude of the moon's orbit never varies over long periods of time.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval assumption of lunar orbital stability. Long-term variations weren't recognized.

era_believed.statement: Planetary motions consist entirely of combinations of uniform circular movements.
era_believed.quality_note: Platonic-Ptolemaic principle of celestial perfection requiring circular uniformity.

era_believed.statement: The equant point in Ptolemaic astronomy represents a real physical mechanism.
era_believed.quality_note: Many medieval astronomers accepted the equant as more than a mathematical device.

era_believed.statement: Mercury and Venus orbit entirely below the Sun in the celestial hierarchy.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Ptolemaic planetary ordering based on their limited elongation from the Sun.

era_believed.statement: Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn orbit above the Sun in the celestial order.
era_believed.quality_note: Ptolemaic superior planet ordering that was universally accepted.

era_believed.statement: Each celestial motion is produced by a soul or intelligence that guides its sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian celestial psychology explaining the source of perfectly uniform motions.

era_believed.statement: The void between atoms is impossible because space must be filled with substance.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian horror vacui principle applied to atomic theory criticism.

era_believed.statement: The natural state of terrestrial matter is rest, and motion always requires an active cause.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian physics distinguishing natural rest from violent motion.

era_believed.statement: Vision requires both incoming light from objects and an active visual faculty within the eye.
era_believed.quality_note: Compromise medieval vision theory attempting to explain active aspects of perception.

era_believed.statement: The eye contains a visual spirit that cooperates with incoming light to produce sight.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval vision theory attempting to reconcile emission and intromission theories.

era_believed.statement: Colors exist as real qualities in objects independent of the perceiving eye.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval color theory treating color as objective material property rather than perceptual phenomenon.

era_believed.statement: The speed of sound and light are both immeasurably fast for practical purposes.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval observational limitation making both seem instantaneous with available methods.

era_believed.statement: Light travels faster in denser media such as water than in rarer media like air.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval optical theory prediction that was contrary to the modern understanding.

era_believed.statement: The retina plays no significant role in the visual process.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval vision theory focused on the crystalline lens rather than recognizing retinal function.

era_believed.statement: The lens of the eye directly senses light rather than merely refracting it.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval understanding of the lens as the primary sensing organ within the eye.

era_believed.statement: Optic nerves are hollow tubes through which visual spirit flows between the brain and eyes.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic neuroanatomical theory explaining visual transmission through pneumatic flow.

era_believed.statement: The heart has three chambers through which blood and vital spirit are processed.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic cardiac anatomy that was standard medieval medical doctrine.

era_believed.statement: Arteries carry vital spirit rather than blood from the heart throughout the body.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic physiology distinguishing between venous blood and arterial vital spirit.

era_believed.statement: The kidneys attract waste from blood through a selective natural faculty.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic physiology explaining kidney function through attractive faculty rather than filtration.

era_believed.statement: The brain produces phlegm which drains downward through the nasal passages.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic physiology explaining nasal discharge through humoral brain secretion.

era_believed.statement: The liver transforms food into blood through a natural heating and concoction process.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic digestive physiology describing blood formation as cooking-like transformation.

era_believed.statement: The heart generates innate heat that sustains body temperature and life processes.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian-Galenic cardiac physiology making the heart the source of bodily heat.

era_believed.statement: The speed of a falling body increases gradually until it reaches its natural maximum velocity.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian physics describing terminal velocity in terms of natural motion limits.

era_believed.statement: The universe is roughly fifty million miles in total radius from Earth to the outermost sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval finite cosmos estimate based on accumulated Ptolemaic distance values.

era_believed.statement: The atmosphere extends upward only a few miles before giving way to the sphere of fire.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval atmospheric theory with Aristotelian elemental layering vastly underestimating atmosphere extent.

era_believed.statement: The mathematical point has real physical existence in the structure of matter.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geometric philosophy treating mathematical abstractions as physically instantiated.

era_believed.statement: The continuum cannot be composed of indivisible atomic points or particles.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian continuum theory used to argue against atomic composition of matter.

era_believed.statement: Every simple body has exactly one natural motion appropriate to its essential nature.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian physics linking elemental identity with characteristic motional behavior.

era_believed.statement: The Earth is only a few thousand years old according to traditional chronologies.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal pre-modern assumption based on religious and philosophical chronologies of creation.

modern_true.statement: The compound microscope was invented in the Netherlands around 1590.
William Harvey described the full circulation of blood through the body in 1628.
Isaac Newton demonstrated that white light is composed of a spectrum of colors in 1672.
The planet Uranus was discovered by William Herschel in 1781.
Eli Whitney patented the cotton gin in 1794.
Edward Jenner developed the first successful smallpox vaccine in 1796.
The speed of light was first measured with reasonable accuracy by Ole Rømer in 1676.
Johannes Kepler published his three laws of planetary motion between 1609 and 1619.
Galileo Galilei observed the four largest moons of Jupiter through a telescope in 1610.
Robert Hooke first described biological cells in his 1665 work Micrographia.
Antoine Lavoisier identified oxygen as the element essential to combustion in the 1770s.
The law of universal gravitation was formulated by Isaac Newton and published in 1687.
The printing press using movable metal type was developed by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440.
The Ottoman Empire conquered Constantinople in 1453.
Christopher Columbus reached the Caribbean islands in 1492.
Vasco da Gama sailed from Portugal to India around the Cape of Good Hope in 1498.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the Earth in 1522.
The logarithm was invented independently by John Napier and Jost Bürgi in the early 1600s.
René Descartes introduced the Cartesian coordinate system in 1637.
Blaise Pascal and Pierre de Fermat founded probability theory through correspondence in 1654.
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz and Isaac Newton independently developed calculus in the late 1600s.
The Mongol Empire conquered Baghdad and destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate in 1258.
The Black Death killed an estimated one-third of Europe's population in the 1340s.
Nicolaus Copernicus proposed a heliocentric model of the solar system in 1543.
Andreas Vesalius published detailed anatomical illustrations of the human body in 1543.
Tycho Brahe compiled the most accurate pre-telescopic astronomical observations in the late 1500s.
The telescope was first constructed in the Netherlands around 1608.
Galileo confirmed the phases of Venus, supporting the heliocentric model, in 1610.
The barometer was invented by Evangelista Torricelli in 1643.
Robert Boyle established the inverse relationship between gas pressure and volume in 1662.
Antonie van Leeuwenhoek observed bacteria through a microscope in the 1670s.
The binary number system was formalized by Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz in 1703.
Benjamin Franklin demonstrated the electrical nature of lightning in 1752.
James Watt patented a greatly improved steam engine in 1769.
Joseph Priestley independently isolated oxygen gas in 1774.
The element hydrogen was identified as a distinct substance by Henry Cavendish in 1766.
The planet Neptune was discovered through mathematical prediction in 1846.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species describing natural selection in 1859.
Gregor Mendel established the fundamental laws of genetic inheritance in 1865.
Louis Pasteur demonstrated that microorganisms cause fermentation and disease in the 1860s.
Dmitri Mendeleev published the first widely recognized periodic table of elements in 1869.
James Clerk Maxwell unified electricity and magnetism into a single theory in the 1860s.
Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1895.
Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity in 1896.
Marie Curie isolated the radioactive elements polonium and radium in 1898.
Albert Einstein published the special theory of relativity in 1905.
Max Planck introduced the quantum hypothesis of energy in 1900.
Ernest Rutherford discovered the atomic nucleus through gold foil experiments in 1911.
Niels Bohr proposed a quantized model of the hydrogen atom in 1913.
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928.
The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932.
Nuclear fission of uranium was first achieved by Otto Hahn and Fritz Strassmann in 1938.
The structure of DNA was determined by Watson and Crick in 1953.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik, was launched into orbit in 1957.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human to travel into outer space in 1961.
Humans first landed on the Moon during the Apollo 11 mission in 1969.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN in 2012.
Gravitational waves were directly detected by LIGO in 2015.
The Spanish Inquisition was established by Ferdinand and Isabella in 1478.
The Aztec Empire was conquered by Hernán Cortés and his forces in 1521.
The Inca Empire was conquered by Francisco Pizarro in 1533.
The Suez Canal was opened connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea in 1869.
The Panama Canal was completed connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans in 1914.
Australia was first mapped by European explorers in the early 1600s.
Antarctica was first sighted by human explorers in 1820.
The French Revolution began with the storming of the Bastille in 1789.
The United States declared independence from Britain in 1776.
The transatlantic telegraph cable was successfully laid in 1866.
The first controlled powered airplane flight was achieved by the Wright brothers in 1903.
Insulin was first used to treat diabetes in humans in 1922.
The first electronic general-purpose computer, ENIAC, became operational in 1945.
The smallpox virus was declared globally eradicated by the World Health Organization in 1980.
Ibn al-Nafis described pulmonary circulation of blood in the thirteenth century.
Nasir al-Din al-Tusi developed the Tusi couple, a mathematical device for planetary models, around 1247.
Roger Bacon described magnifying lenses and predicted flying machines in the thirteenth century.
Snell's law of refraction was mathematically formulated by Willebrord Snellius in 1621.
The wave theory of light was proposed by Christiaan Huygens in 1678.
Thomas Young demonstrated light interference patterns supporting the wave theory in 1801.
Augustin-Jean Fresnel developed a comprehensive mathematical wave theory of light in the 1810s.
The electromagnetic nature of light was established by James Clerk Maxwell in 1865.
The photoelectric effect was explained by Albert Einstein using light quanta in 1905.
Laser light was first produced in a laboratory in 1960.
Fiber optic cables now transmit information as pulses of light across ocean floors.
Non-Euclidean geometry was independently developed by Lobachevsky and Bolyai in the 1830s.
Georg Cantor established set theory and proved different sizes of infinity exist in the 1870s.
The general theory of relativity describes gravity as curvature of spacetime caused by mass.
Bernhard Riemann developed the geometry later used by Einstein for general relativity in 1854.
Leonhard Euler introduced the number e and the function notation f(x) in the 1700s.
The four-color map theorem was proven with computer assistance in 1976.
Andrew Wiles proved Fermat's Last Theorem in 1995.
Germ theory replaced miasma theory as the accepted explanation for infectious disease in the late 1800s.
Joseph Lister introduced antiseptic surgical techniques using carbolic acid in 1867.
The first successful human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1967.
The human genome was fully sequenced for the first time in 2003.
CRISPR gene-editing technology was adapted for use in human cells in 2013.
Anesthesia using ether was first publicly demonstrated in surgery in 1846.
Blood types were discovered by Karl Landsteiner in 1901.
The electron was discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897.
Superconductivity was discovered by Heike Kamerlingh Onnes in 1911.
The cosmic microwave background radiation was detected by Penzias and Wilson in 1965.
The expansion of the universe was observationally confirmed by Edwin Hubble in 1929.
Dark energy was inferred from observations of accelerating cosmic expansion in 1998.
Exoplanets orbiting other stars were first confirmed in the 1990s.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft entered interstellar space in 2012.
The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous land empire in recorded history.
Gunpowder weapons fundamentally transformed warfare in Europe and Asia after the thirteenth century.
The magnetic compass was widely adopted for ocean navigation in medieval Europe and China.
The mechanical clock was developed in Europe in the late thirteenth century.
The germ responsible for tuberculosis was identified by Robert Koch in 1882.
Electricity was harnessed for widespread public lighting in cities in the 1880s.
The first permanent photograph was produced by Nicéphore Niépce in 1826.
Radio waves were first transmitted and received by Heinrich Hertz in 1887.
The transistor was invented at Bell Laboratories in 1947.
The internet originated from the ARPANET project first connected in 1969.
Plate tectonics was accepted as the unifying theory of geology in the 1960s.
The ozone layer in Earth's stratosphere was discovered by Charles Fabry in 1913.
Alfred Wegener proposed the theory of continental drift in 1912.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered in Egypt by French soldiers in 1799.
The thermometer was developed into a reliable scientific instrument in the early 1700s.
Absolute zero was established as the lowest possible temperature at minus 273.15 degrees Celsius.

modern_false.statement: Ibn al-Haytham completed his Book of Optics in 1028 while living in Damascus under Fatimid patronage.
The law of refraction was first mathematically formulated by Kamal al-Din al-Farisi in 1295.
Roger Bacon invented the magnifying glass in 1261 at the University of Paris.
Alhazen demonstrated the corpuscular theory of light using a rotating mirror apparatus in 1037.
The camera obscura was first described in writing by Robert Grosseteste in 1224.
Witelo's Perspectiva was published in 1268 and contained the first correct explanation of the rainbow.
Theodoric of Freiberg explained total internal reflection in his treatise of 1311.
Al-Biruni calculated the Earth's circumference in 1055 with an error of less than two percent using a method involving lunar eclipses.
The first known pair of corrective eyeglasses was crafted in Venice in 1279 by Salvino degli Armati.
Fibonacci introduced the Hindu-Arabic numeral system to Europe in his Liber Abaci published in 1198.
Omar Khayyam solved the general cubic equation algebraically in 1079 using methods involving conic sections.
Nasir al-Din al-Tusi published his coupled-circle theorem as an alternative to Ptolemaic equants in 1247.
Jordanus de Nemore proved the law of the lever using virtual displacements in 1260 at the University of Bologna.
The first European translation of Euclid's Elements from Arabic was completed by Adelard of Bath in 1136.
Gerard of Cremona translated Ptolemy's Almagest into Latin in 1187 in Seville.
Nicole Oresme developed a graphical method for representing velocity in 1358 at the University of Oxford.
Thomas Bradwardine formulated his law relating force, resistance, and velocity in 1328 at Merton College.
Jean Buridan proposed the impetus theory of motion in 1340 as a direct refutation of Aristotelian physics.
The mean speed theorem was first proved by William Heytesbury in 1345 at the University of Paris.
Albert of Saxony correctly described projectile motion as following a curved path in 1372.
The Hindu mathematician Bhaskara II proved the Pythagorean theorem using calculus-like methods in 1158.
Al-Samawal ibn Yahya al-Maghribi developed the binomial theorem for integer exponents in 1180.
The Chinese mathematician Zhu Shijie published his Jade Mirror of the Four Unknowns in 1299 in Nanjing.
Li Ye introduced negative numbers into polynomial equations in his Sea Mirror of Circle Measurements of 1248.
The first mechanical clock driven by weights was built in Milan in 1277.
Villard de Honnecourt designed a perpetual motion machine using mercury wheels around 1240 that was briefly tested.
The magnetic compass was first used for maritime navigation in Europe in 1187 by Genoese sailors.
Peter Peregrinus wrote his letter on the magnet in 1279 while stationed at the siege of Lucera.
Albertus Magnus successfully isolated arsenic in elemental form in 1256 at his laboratory in Cologne.
The first European paper mill was established in Fabriano, Italy, in 1264.
Gunpowder was first used in European warfare at the Battle of Crécy in 1346 by English forces.
The earliest known European cannon was deployed at the siege of Metz in 1324.
Marco Polo returned to Venice in 1298 and brought the first samples of printed Chinese paper currency to Europe.
The Black Death reached Constantinople in 1345 before spreading to Sicily in 1347.
The University of Oxford was formally chartered by King Henry II in 1167.
The University of Naples was founded in 1222 by Pope Honorius III.
The Domesday Book was completed in 1088 under the supervision of William II of England.
The Magna Carta was sealed at Runnymede in 1217 after the First Barons' War.
The Fourth Crusade sacked Constantinople in 1202 before being redirected to Egypt.
The Mongol Empire was formally established by Genghis Khan in 1204 at the kurultai of Burkhan Khaldun.
The printing press using movable metal type was invented by Johannes Gutenberg in 1448 in Mainz.
Copernicus published De Revolutionibus Orbium Coelestium in 1541 while living in Kraków.
Vesalius published De Humani Corporis Fabrica in 1545 at the University of Bologna.
William Harvey described the circulation of blood in De Motu Cordis published in 1626.
Galileo first observed the moons of Jupiter in 1608 using a telescope he purchased from a Dutch merchant.
Isaac Newton published the Principia Mathematica in 1685 with financial support from the Royal Society.
The Declaration of Independence was signed on July 2, 1776, in Philadelphia.
The French Revolution began with the storming of the Bastille on July 20, 1789.
Napoleon was defeated at the Battle of Waterloo in 1813 by a coalition led by the Duke of Wellington.
The United States Constitution was ratified in 1786 by nine of the thirteen original states.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1861 after returning from the Galápagos Islands.
The periodic table of elements was first published by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1867.
Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone in 1878 after a legal dispute with Elisha Gray.
Thomas Edison invented the incandescent light bulb in 1877 at his laboratory in Newark, New Jersey.
The Wright brothers achieved the first powered flight on December 17, 1901, at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.
Albert Einstein published his special theory of relativity in 1903 while working at the Swiss Patent Office.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1926 at University College London.
The Titanic sank on April 15, 1914, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic.
World War I ended with the signing of the armistice on November 11, 1919.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1920 at the Palace of Versailles.
Women gained the right to vote in the United States with the Nineteenth Amendment in 1918.
The stock market crash that triggered the Great Depression occurred on October 29, 1931.
The Empire State Building was completed in 1929 and was the tallest building in the world for forty years.
Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1942, bringing the United States into World War II.
The atomic bomb was first tested at the Trinity site in New Mexico on July 16, 1944.
The United Nations was officially established on October 24, 1947, in San Francisco.
The Berlin Wall was constructed in August 1959 to prevent East Germans from fleeing to the West.
The Cuban Missile Crisis occurred in October 1964 during the presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson.
Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1971.
The Watergate scandal led to the resignation of President Richard Nixon on August 9, 1976.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1988, in the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1991, leading to the reunification of Germany.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1987 at MIT.
The Human Genome Project was completed in 2001 under the direction of Francis Collins at the NIH.
The September 11 attacks on the World Trade Center occurred in 2003.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in 1992 aboard the Space Shuttle Discovery.
The first iPhone was released by Apple in 2005 and revolutionized the smartphone industry.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN began operations in 2010 near Geneva, Switzerland.
The Higgs boson was confirmed experimentally at CERN in 2014 using data from the ATLAS detector.
SpaceX successfully landed a reusable orbital rocket booster for the first time in 2017.
The COVID-19 pandemic was officially declared by the World Health Organization on March 11, 2019.
The Paris Climate Agreement was signed in 2017 by 192 countries at the United Nations Climate Summit.
Bitcoin was created in 2007 by an anonymous developer using the pseudonym Satoshi Nakamoto.
The Mars rover Curiosity landed on Mars on August 6, 2014, in Jezero Crater.
CRISPR gene-editing technology was first demonstrated by Jennifer Doudna and Feng Zhang in 2010.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft entered interstellar space in 2015 after traveling for over thirty-six years.
The first successful cloning of a mammal produced Dolly the sheep in 1998 at the Roslin Institute.
The International Space Station began continuous habitation in 2002 with a crew of four astronauts.
Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet by the International Astronomical Union in 2004.
The discovery of gravitational waves was announced by LIGO in 2014 confirming Einstein's prediction.
The Kepler Space Telescope was launched in 2011 to search for exoplanets orbiting distant stars.
The first photograph of a black hole was released in 2021 by the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration.
Roger Penrose won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2018 for his work on black hole singularities.
The element oganesson was first synthesized in 2004 at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna.
The Rosetta spacecraft successfully landed on comet 67P in 2016 after a ten-year journey.
Marie Curie was awarded her second Nobel Prize in 1913 for her work on radioactive isotopes.
The Panama Canal was completed and opened to traffic in 1916 under American administration.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1945 by William Shockley and John Bardeen.
The double helix structure of DNA was described by Watson and Crick in 1951 at Cambridge University.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1959.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 12, 1963, aboard the Vostok 1 spacecraft.
The laser was first demonstrated by Theodore Maiman in 1958 at Hughes Research Laboratories.
The polio vaccine was developed by Jonas Salk and first administered widely in 1957.
The discovery of cosmic microwave background radiation was made by Penzias and Wilson in 1967.
The first heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1969 in Cape Town, South Africa.
The Apollo 13 mission experienced a critical failure in 1972 but returned safely to Earth.
The Concorde supersonic airliner made its first commercial flight in 1978 between London and New York.
The Suez Canal was nationalized by Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser in 1958.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted by the United Nations General Assembly in 1950.
India gained independence from British rule on August 15, 1949, under the leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru.
The Korean War ended with an armistice signed on July 27, 1955, at Panmunjom.
The European Economic Community was established by the Treaty of Rome in 1959.
The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg in 1462 in Strasbourg.
Nicolaus Copernicus published De revolutionibus orbium coelestium in 1551 in Kraków.
The telescope was first patented by Hans Lippershey in 1611 in the Dutch Republic.
Isaac Newton published Principia Mathematica in 1694 through the Royal Society of London.
Galileo Galilei first observed the moons of Jupiter in 1614 using a refracting telescope.
The logarithm was invented by John Napier in 1621 and published in Edinburgh.
William Harvey described the circulation of blood in 1637 in his treatise De Motu Cordis.
Robert Hooke coined the term "cell" in 1672 after observing cork under a microscope.
